title: Staff+ ML System Design
description: [Intuition]: Staff+ ML system design at FAANG goes beyond building a single model — you design the ML platform, infrastructure, and process that enables multiple teams to build, deploy, and maintain ML systems reliably at scale. The key distinction from Senior-level design is scope: you're not designing a recommendation system; you're designing the platform on which 10 teams build recommendation systems, search ranking, fraud detection, and more. This file covers 12 Staff+ scope problems with production tradeoffs, capacity planning, and FAANG-level architecture decisions.
details: [Objectives]: Master Staff+ ML system design by solving 12 problems at platform/infrastructure scope. Each problem covers requirements clarification, architecture, data pipeline, model training, serving, deployment, monitoring, and tradeoff analysis. Designed to match Google L6/Meta E6/Amazon L6 SD interview expectations.

sections.title: Design an ML Platform (Feature Store + Training + Serving)
sections.description:
[Intuition] An ML platform is the internal product that makes every other ML system possible. The feature store is central: it's a unified layer where data scientists register feature definitions, compute transformations (batch + streaming), and serve feature vectors at training time (with point-in-time correct joins) and at serving time (low-latency lookup). Without a feature store, each team builds their own feature engineering pipeline — duplicated work, inconsistent features, no governance.

[Concrete Numbers] Feature store at Uber (Michelangelo): 1000s of features, 100+ teams, 10M+ QPS serving, sub-10ms p99 feature retrieval latency. Training data generation: point-in-time joins across 100s of feature tables, handling backfills of up to 6 months. Storage: offline (Parquet on S3/HDFS — daily snapshots) + online (RocksDB/Cassandra — latest value for real-time).

[Practical Workflow] 1. Data scientist defines a feature via YAML manifest (name, type, source SQL/stream, schedule). 2. CI pipeline validates the feature definition (schema check, backfill test, distribution shift test). 3. Feature computation runs on Spark (batch) or Flink (streaming). 4. Offline store updated; online store populated via key-value sync. 5. Training: point-in-time join produces labeled dataset. 6. Serving: low-latency feature lookup from online store via gRPC.

[Interview Angle] Google L6: "Design an ML platform for a company with 50 ML engineers across 10 teams." Start with the feature store as the foundation — it solves the most painful problem (inconsistent features between training and serving). Then layer on training infrastructure, model registry, and serving infrastructure. Key tradeoff: centralized vs self-serve — a centralized platform team maintains the platform, but each team owns their model lifecycle.
sections.codeBlock:
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional, List
from datetime import datetime

# Feature Store API abstraction
# Central interface for defining, computing, and serving features

@dataclass
class FeatureDefinition:
    name: str
    feature_group: str      # e.g. 'user_features', 'item_features'
    dtype: str              # 'float', 'int', 'string', 'vector<float>'
    source_sql: str         # SQL query that defines the feature
    compute_mode: str       # 'batch' (Spark) or 'streaming' (Flink)
    freshness_sla_sec: int  # Max age of feature value for serving
    owner: str              # Team that owns this feature

    def validate(self) -> bool:
        """CI check: validate feature definition before deployment."""
        checks = [
            self.name.isidentifier(),
            self.dtype in ('float', 'int', 'string', 'vector<float>'),
            self.freshness_sla_sec > 0,
            bool(self.owner),
        ]
        return all(checks)

class OnlineFeatureService:
    """Low-latency feature serving via gRPC."""
    def __init__(self, store_backend: str = 'rocksdb'):
        self._store = {}  # In practice: RocksDB/Cassandra with TTL

    async def get_features(self, entity_key: str, feature_names: List[str]):
        """Batch feature lookup for a single entity."""
        vector = {}
        for name in feature_names:
            val = self._store.get(f'{entity_key}:{name}')
            if val is not None:
                vector[name] = val
        return vector

    def bulk_get(self, entity_keys: List[str], feature_names: List[str]):
        """Batch feature lookup for multiple entities."""
        return [self.get_features(key, feature_names) for key in entity_keys]

sections.title: Design a Real-Time ML System at 10M+ QPS
sections.description:
[Intuition] Real-time ML at 10M+ QPS means every millisecond matters — you can't run an expensive model on every request. The architecture must split the work: lightweight ensemble of shallow models (logistic regression, small decision trees) for the critical path, with async enrichment from deep models when there's headroom. Streaming vs batch: streaming features are processed on-the-fly from events (user just clicked X), while batch features are pre-computed hourly/daily. The streaming path needs a dedicated low-latency pipeline (Kafka → Flink → online feature store), while the batch path is Spark → offline store.

[Concrete Numbers] At 10M QPS with p99 latency 20ms: you need ~200K requests/second per CPU core (assuming 4ms per request), so ~50 cores for the critical path. Memory: feature cache of 100M entities × 10 features × 8 bytes = 8GB for a single replica. Network bandwidth: each request is ~1KB → 10GB/s inbound, 10GB/s outbound. You'll need 20+ replicas behind a load balancer (each handling 500K QPS).

[Practical Workflow] 1. Profile your serving path: measure p50/p95/p99 latency for each component. 2. Identify the bottleneck (typically feature retrieval or model inference). 3. Cache aggressively: local LRU cache for hot features, updated via Kafka consumer. 4. Batch inference requests: group 16 requests → single batch prediction → split results. 5. Circuit break the deep model path: if async enrichment fails, serve the shallow ensemble result directly.

[Interview Angle] Google L6: "Design a real-time fraud detection system for Google Pay." You need sub-100ms decision latency at 1M+ TPS. The answer is an ML microservice with three model tiers: Tier 1 (rules + LR, <5ms), Tier 2 (XGBoost, <20ms), Tier 3 (async GNN, <200ms). Most requests resolved at Tier 1.
sections.codeBlock:
import asyncio
import numpy as np
from typing import Dict, List, Optional

class RealTimePredictionPipeline:
    """Async prediction pipeline for real-time ML at 10M+ QPS.
    ponytail: synchronous fallback if async deep model fails.
    """
    def __init__(self):
        self.cache: Dict[str, float] = {}  # LRU feature cache (simplified)
        self.shallow_model = None  # Logistic regression
        self.deep_model = None     # Async transformer

    async def predict(self, features: Dict) -> float:
        """Critical path: shallow model prediction."""
        # Fast feature lookup (cache hit >95% for hot features)
        x = np.array([features.get(k, 0.0) for k in ['f1', 'f2', 'f3', 'f4']])
        score = self.shallow_model.predict_proba(x.reshape(1, -1))[0, 1]
        # Async enrichment — fire and forget
        asyncio.create_task(self._async_deep_enrichment(features, score))
        return score

    async def _async_deep_enrichment(self, features: Dict, shallow_score: float):
        """Non-blocking deep model evaluation. Results logged for training."""
        try:
            deep_score = await asyncio.wait_for(
                self.deep_model.predict(features), timeout=0.05
            )
            # Log discrepancy for training data quality monitoring
            self._log_gap(shallow_score, deep_score)
        except asyncio.TimeoutError:
            # Circuit breaker: shallow score is sufficient
            pass

    def _log_gap(self, shallow: float, deep: float) -> None:
        """Log prediction gap for monitoring and potential retraining."""
        gap = abs(shallow - deep)
        if gap > 0.2:
            print(f'ALERT: Large prediction gap: {gap:.3f}')

sections.title: Design a Multi-Stage Ranking Pipeline (Retrieval → Pre-Ranking → Ranking → Re-Ranking)
sections.description:
[Intuition] The funnel architecture is how every large-scale ranking system works: candidate retrieval (cheap, high recall, 10K items) → pre-ranking (lightweight model, 1K items) → ranking (deep model, 100 items) → re-ranking (heuristics and diversity, 10 items). Each stage reduces items and increases model complexity. The total system cost = sum of (items × cost per item) at each stage. By pruning at each stage, the most expensive model only runs on ~1% of the original candidates.

[Concrete Numbers] Retrieval: 10K items, model-free (ANN index, <1ms). Pre-ranking: 1K items, small DNN (2 layers, 64 units, <5ms). Ranking: 100 items, deep DNN with cross features (8 layers, 1024 units with attention, <50ms). Re-ranking: 10 items, heuristic rules (MMR diversity, business constraints, <1ms). Cost ratio: if ranking 1 item costs $1, retrieval is $0.01, pre-ranking is $0.10, re-ranking is $0.02. Total cost: ~$20 for 100 ranked items vs $100 if you ranked all 10K.

[Practical Workflow] 1. Deploy new retrieval index (HNSW with updated embeddings). 2. Shadow the new pre-ranking model for 2 days (compare pre-ranking scores vs full ranking). 3. Canary the new ranking model at 5% traffic — if NDCG@10 improves by 1%+ and latency stays under 50ms p99, proceed to 100%. 4. Verify re-ranking rules offline: diversity metrics (intra-list similarity) and business metrics (ad revenue impact). 5. Monitor end-to-end: E2E latency p99 <200ms, system throughput stable.

[Interview Angle] Meta E6: "Design the ranking pipeline for Facebook Feed." The funnel architecture is expected — the key insight is that each stage has different optimization objectives. Retrieval optimizes recall@10K, pre-ranking optimizes recall@1K (with distillation from the deep ranker), ranking optimizes NDCG@100, and re-ranking optimizes user engagement metrics (diversity, freshness). Distillation from the deep ranker to the pre-ranking model is critical — otherwise the pre-ranking model learns from its own errors.
sections.codeBlock:
import numpy as np
import xgboost as xgb
from typing import List, Tuple

class MultiStageRankingPipeline:
    """Funnel: retrieval → pre-rank → rank → re-rank."""
    def __init__(self, num_candidates: int = 10000):
        self.num_candidates = num_candidates
        self.retrieval_model = None  # HNSW / two-tower
        self.pre_rank_model = None   # LightGBM or small DNN (distilled)
        self.rank_model = None       # XGBoost with cross features

    async def retrieve(self, query_emb: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
        """Stage 1: ANN retrieval — return 10K item IDs."""
        # In practice: HNSW approximate nearest neighbor
        return np.random.randint(0, 1_000_000, size=self.num_candidates)

    def pre_rank(self, item_ids: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
        """Stage 2: Pre-rank with distilled model — reduce to 1K."""
        pre_rank_scores = self.pre_rank_model.predict(
            self._extract_lightweight_features(item_ids)
        )
        top_k = 1000
        top_indices = np.argsort(-pre_rank_scores)[:top_k]
        return item_ids[top_indices]

    def rank(self, item_ids: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
        """Stage 3: Full ranking with XGBoost ensemble — top 100."""
        features = self._extract_deep_features(item_ids)
        scores = np.mean([
            model.predict(features) for model in self.rank_model
        ], axis=0)  # Ensemble of 3 XGBoost models
        top_k = 100
        top_indices = np.argsort(-scores)[:top_k]
        return item_ids[top_indices]

    def re_rank(self, item_ids: np.ndarray, embeddings: np.ndarray) -> List[int]:
        """Stage 4: MMR diversity + business rules — final 10."""
        selected = []
        candidates = list(range(len(item_ids)))
        lambda_val = 0.6
        for _ in range(10):
            mmr_scores = [
                lambda_val * 1.0 - (1 - lambda_val) * max(
                    [embeddings[i] @ embeddings[s] for s in selected], default=0
                )
                for i in candidates
            ]
            best_idx = candidates[np.argmax(mmr_scores)]
            selected.append(best_idx)
            candidates.remove(best_idx)
        return [item_ids[i] for i in selected]

    def _extract_lightweight_features(self, item_ids):
        """Cheap features: popularity, recency, content type."""
        return np.random.randn(len(item_ids), 16)

    def _extract_deep_features(self, item_ids):
        """Expensive features: cross features, user-item interactions."""
        return np.random.randn(len(item_ids), 128)

sections.title: Design an A/B Experimentation Platform at Scale
sections.description:
[Intuition] A/B experimentation at scale is fundamentally harder than textbook statistics because of interference. In a recommendation system, you can't treat users as independent: if User A gets a different feed, it changes what content is available for User B (network effects). If you change the bidding algorithm for 10% of ad auctions, it changes the market dynamics for everyone (market interference). A Staff+ platform must handle network exposure models, switchback experiments for two-sided markets, and variance reduction techniques like CUPED (Controlled-experiment Using Pre-Experiment Data).

[Concrete Numbers] Traffic allocation: typical A/B test needs 100K users per variant for 0.5% MDE (minimum detectable effect). CUPED reduces variance by 40-60%, so you need only 40-60K users. For switchback experiments (e.g., marketplace pricing), you need minimum 2-week windows with 50-50 time split. Experiment velocity: Facebook runs 10K+ concurrent experiments. The platform must manage overlapping experiments with SRM (Sample Ratio Mismatch) detection.

[Practical Workflow] 1. Define hypothesis and metric (primary + guardrail metrics). 2. Choose experiment type: user-randomized (most cases), switchback (marketplace), or network-randomized (social features). 3. Allocate traffic via deterministic hashing (consistent for the experiment duration). 4. Run for minimum duration (typically 1-2 weeks — weekend effect matters). 5. Apply CUPED to reduce variance. 6. Check SRM (χ² test), then analyze uplift. 7. If significant (p < 0.05) and practically significant (effect > MDE), proceed to full rollout.

[Interview Angle] Netflix Staff: "Design an A/B testing platform for Netflix's recommendation system." The key challenge is interference: changing recommendations for some users affects content popularity metrics for other users. Answer: use cluster-randomized experiments (randomize at the content cluster level, not user level), or use switchbacks for the personalization algorithm. Also design guardrails: don't let experiments run forever (auto-stop at 4 weeks).
sections.codeBlock:
import numpy as np
from scipy import stats
from typing import Dict, List

class CUPED:
    """CUPED variance reduction for A/B tests.
    Uses pre-experiment data (covariate) to reduce metric variance.
    ponytail: assumes linear relationship; non-linear extensions exist.
    """
    def __init__(self, treatment: np.ndarray, control: np.ndarray,
                 pre_treat: np.ndarray, pre_ctrl: np.ndarray):
        self.treatment = treatment
        self.control = control
        self.pre_treat = pre_treat
        self.pre_ctrl = pre_ctrl

    @staticmethod
    def _theta(Y_pre, Y):
        """Optimal scaling factor theta = Cov(Y_pre, Y) / Var(Y_pre)."""
        cov = np.cov(Y_pre, Y)[0, 1]
        var = np.var(Y_pre) + 1e-8
        return cov / var

    def adjust(self):
        """Apply CUPED: Y_adj = Y - theta * (Y_pre - mu_pre)."""
        theta_t = self._theta(self.pre_treat, self.treatment)
        theta_c = self._theta(self.pre_ctrl, self.control)
        # Use pooled theta for unbiased estimate
        theta = (theta_t + theta_c) / 2
        mu_pre = np.mean(np.concatenate([self.pre_treat, self.pre_ctrl]))
        t_adj = self.treatment - theta * (self.pre_treat - mu_pre)
        c_adj = self.control - theta * (self.pre_ctrl - mu_pre)
        return t_adj, c_adj

    def analyze(self):
        """Run CUPED-adjusted A/B test."""
        t_adj, c_adj = self.adjust()
        t_stat, p_val = stats.ttest_ind(t_adj, c_adj)
        lift = (np.mean(t_adj) - np.mean(c_adj)) / np.mean(c_adj)
        return {'lift': lift, 'p_value': p_val, 'significant': p_val < 0.05}

sections.title: Design a Distributed Training Platform
sections.description:
[Intuition] Training large models requires parallelism across multiple axes. Data parallelism replicates the model on each GPU and splits the batch; each GPU computes gradients independently and all-reduces them. Tensor parallelism splits individual layers across GPUs (so a single matrix multiply is distributed). Pipeline parallelism splits layers across GPUs (layer 1-4 on GPU 0, 5-8 on GPU 1, etc). Expert parallelism (MoE) routes each token to different experts on different GPUs. The art is choosing the right combination. For a 100B parameter model: you use 3D parallelism (data + tensor + pipeline) across 256+ GPUs.

[Concrete Numbers] Llama-2-70B training: 1720 NVIDIA A100-80GB GPUs, ~0.5% efficient (MFU). Data parallelism: 1-2 TB/s all-reduce bandwidth (NVLink 600GB/s within node, InfiniBand 400Gb/s across nodes). Tensor parallelism within node (8 GPUs), pipeline parallelism across nodes (4-8 nodes per pipeline group). Memory: model weights (70B × 2 bytes = 140GB in BF16), optimizer states (140GB × 2 = 280GB for Adam), activations (depends on batch size and sequence length). FSDP (Fully Sharded Data Parallel) shards optimizer states, gradients, and parameters across GPUs — each GPU holds only 1/N of the total.

[Practical Workflow] 1. Estimate memory: model weights + optimizer + activations + gradients. 2. Choose parallelism strategy: if model fits on a single GPU with FSDP, use data parallelism only. If not, start with tensor parallelism within node (for Transformer layers), then pipeline parallelism across nodes. 3. Configure the launcher: set world_size, rank mapping, and topology. 4. Profile communication overhead: use torch.profiler to find the bottleneck. 5. Tune micro-batch size for pipeline balance. 6. Enable mixed precision (BF16) and activation checkpointing.

[Interview Angle] Google L6: "Design a training platform for 100B+ parameter models." Start with FSDP for simplicity (shards model state across GPUs, compute is same as data parallelism). Then upgrade to 3D parallelism when model exceeds GPU memory per node. Key insight: the bottleneck is communication, not computation — you want to overlap compute and communication (e.g., prefetch next layer's parameters while computing current layer).
sections.codeBlock:
# torch.distributed.launch configuration for 3D parallelism
# Example: 256 GPUs across 32 nodes (8 GPUs/node)

# FSDP (Fully Sharded Data Parallel) config
fsdp_config = {
    'sharding_strategy': 'FULL_SHARD',          # Shard params, grads, optimizer states
    'cpu_offload': False,                        # Keep all on GPU for speed
    'mixed_precision': {
        'param_dtype': 'torch.bfloat16',
        'reduce_dtype': 'torch.float32',
        'buffer_dtype': 'torch.float32',
    },
    'backward_prefetch': 'BACKWARD_PRE',         # Prefetch next shard during backward
    'forward_prefetch': True,                    # Prefetch next shard during forward
    'limit_all_gathers': True,                   # Limit simultaneous all-gather calls
    'activation_checkpointing': True,            # Trades compute for memory
}

# torchrun launcher command (simplified)
# torchrun --nproc_per_node=8 --nnodes=32 --rdzv_endpoint=master:1234 \
#     --max_restarts=3 train.py --model_size=100b --batch_size=4

# Within training script:
# from torch.distributed.fsdp import FullyShardedDataParallel as FSDP
# model = FSDP(model, **fsdp_config)
#
# Optional: Tensor Parallelism via device mesh (PyTorch 2.0+ DTensor)
# from torch.distributed.tensor.parallel import parallelize_module
# Parallelize attention: split heads across 8 GPUs within node
# Parallelize FFN: split intermediate dimension across 8 GPUs

sections.title: Design a Data Labeling & Quality Platform
sections.description:
[Intuition] At Staff+ scope, you design the labeling platform that enables multiple teams to produce high-quality labels efficiently. Three complementary approaches: (1) Active learning — the model selects the most uncertain/valuable examples for human labeling, minimizing labeling cost. (2) Weak supervision (Snorkel/Skweak) — domain experts write labeling functions (heuristic rules that assign labels with noise), and the platform estimates their accuracy and combines them into a single probabilistic label. (3) Human-in-the-loop — human labelers handle the examples that the model is uncertain about. The platform must track labeler agreement, estimate label quality, and detect labeler drift.

[Concrete Numbers] Cost per label (2024): simple classification (sentiment, intent) $0.01-0.05/label, bounding boxes $0.10-0.50/image, medical annotation $1-5/image, expert annotation (legal, medical) $10-100+/sample. Active learning reduces cost by 50-80%: you only label the 20% of examples that the model is most uncertain about. Labeler agreement: target Cohen's κ > 0.7 for most tasks. Labeler throughput: 100-500 simple labels/hour per labeler.

[Practical Workflow] 1. Data scientists write labeling functions in Python (classes inheriting from a LabelingFunction base class). 2. CI runs the labeling functions on an unlabeled pool and estimates their accuracy using generative model (Snorkel's label model). 3. For examples with low-confidence labels (label model probability < 0.7), dispatch to human labelers via the platform. 4. Human labels are collected, inter-annotator agreement is computed. 5. The platform labels are used for training; quality metrics are logged to the model registry.

[Interview Angle] Amazon L6: "Design a data labeling platform for Alexa's ML systems." Amazon has thousands of intents and entities. The key challenge: label consistency across different teams and languages. Answer: a centralized labeling platform with (1) label ontology management, (2) Snorkel-style weak supervision for high-coverage intents, (3) active learning for long-tail intents, (4) labeler qualification tests, and (5) continuous monitoring of label quality.

sections.title: Design Model Lifecycle Management (Governance, Audit, Rollback)
sections.description:
[Intuition] Model lifecycle management is the operating system for ML: it tracks every model from conception to retirement. The model registry is the single source of truth — it stores model versions, metadata (training data hash, hyperparameters, evaluation metrics), lineage (which code + data + config produced this model), and governance (who approved it, when, for what use case). Audit trails are critical for regulated industries (finance, healthcare, EU AI Act). Rollback capabilities require that every deployment is reversible — you store the previous model version and its serving configuration.

[Concrete Numbers] Audit trail requirements (regulatory): keep records for 5+ years, log every prediction for high-risk models, record every model deployment and approval. Storage: model registry metadata ~1KB per version, prediction logs ~100 bytes per prediction (at 1M QPS = 100MB/hour = 2.4TB/day — feasible with sampling). Deployment cadence: 10-100 model deployments per week at a large FAANG team. Rollback must be <5 minutes (automated via Kubernetes + feature flags).

[Practical Workflow] 1. Data scientist registers model version in model registry (automatically captures training run ID, data snapshot hash, hyperparameters). 2. CI/CD runs evaluation suite: accuracy on held-out test set, fairness audit, latency benchmark, data drift check. 3. Model enters staging: requires approval from lead (QA + compliance if regulated). 4. Approved → deployment via feature flag (shadow → canary → full). 5. Monitoring alerts on prediction drift, latency degradation, or error rate spikes. 6. If alert fires, auto-rollback to previous model version — the registry stores the last-known-good version for each deployment.

[Interview Angle] Google/Meta Staff: "Design model governance for a fintech ML system." This is about regulatory compliance: you need (1) complete audit trail for every model prediction, (2) explainability for any decision, (3) data lineage to trace predictions back to training data, (4) fairness monitoring across demographic groups, (5) model approval workflow with sign-off from legal and compliance. The model registry is the central component — everything traces through it.
sections.codeBlock:
# Model registry schema (simplified PostgreSQL DDL)

SCHEMA = """
CREATE TABLE model_versions (
    id              BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    model_name      TEXT NOT NULL,
    version         INT NOT NULL,
    status          TEXT CHECK (status IN ('staging','approved','deployed','deprecated')),
    training_run_id TEXT UNIQUE,
    data_snapshot   TEXT,           -- Hash or path to training data snapshot
    config_json     JSONB,          -- Hyperparameters, feature list
    metrics_json    JSONB,          -- Accuracy, AUC, latency benchmarks
    artifacts_uri   TEXT,           -- S3 path to model weights
    created_by      TEXT NOT NULL,
    created_at      TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW(),
    approved_by     TEXT,
    approved_at     TIMESTAMPTZ,
    deployment_tag  TEXT,           -- 'prod', 'canary', 'shadow'
    rollback_to     INT REFERENCES model_versions(id),
    UNIQUE(model_name, version)
);

-- Audit log: every prediction for regulated use cases
CREATE TABLE prediction_audit_log (
    id              BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    model_version_id INT REFERENCES model_versions(id),
    request_id      TEXT NOT NULL,
    input_hash      TEXT,           -- Hash of input features
    prediction      FLOAT,
    confidence      FLOAT,
    served_at       TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW(),
    decision        TEXT,           -- The business decision made from this prediction
    reviewed_by     TEXT            -- Human review for high-stakes decisions
);

-- Index for audit queries
CREATE INDEX idx_audit_model_time ON prediction_audit_log(model_version_id, served_at);
"""

# Audit log insertion (Python snippet)
def log_prediction(model_version_id: int, request_id: str,
                   prediction: float, input_hash: str):
    """Log a single prediction for audit trail."""
    # INSERT INTO prediction_audit_log ...
    pass

# Auto-rollback check
def check_and_rollback(metric_name: str, current_value: float, threshold: float):
    """If metric exceeds threshold, rollback to previous version."""
    if current_value > threshold:
        # UPDATE model_versions SET deployment_tag = 'rollback' WHERE id = current
        # UPDATE model_versions SET deployment_tag = 'prod' WHERE id = previous
        print(f'AUTO-ROLLBACK: {metric_name}={current_value} > threshold={threshold}')
        return True
    return False

sections.title: Design an Anomaly Detection System at Scale
sections.description:
[Intuition] Anomaly detection at scale means detecting outliers across million of metrics every minute — you can't have a human set thresholds for each one. The system must automatically learn the normal behavior of each metric and flag deviations. Three approaches with different tradeoffs: (1) Statistical methods (Z-score, IQR, MAD, EWMA) — fast, interpretable, good for univariate metrics with known distributions. (2) ML methods (Isolation Forest, One-Class SVM, LOF) — handle multivariate anomalies, capture correlations between metrics. (3) Deep learning (Autoencoders, LSTM-based prediction) — best for complex temporal patterns (seasonality, trends) but expensive to train and serve. A production system uses an ensemble: statistical methods for real-time alarms (<1ms), ML methods for periodic deep scans (every 5 minutes).

[Concrete Numbers] Scale: 1M metrics/minute. Each metric: time series of last 24 hours at 1-minute granularity (1440 data points). Storage: ~1KB per metric per day = 1GB/day for all metrics. Query: need to detect anomalies within 1 minute of the data point arriving. Throughput: 16,666 metric evaluations/second — requires distributed workers (e.g., 16 workers × 1000 metrics/second each). False positive rate: target <1% (otherwise SRE teams get desensitized).

[Practical Workflow] 1. Ingest time-series metrics via Kafka (1M+ messages/min). 2. Batch metrics into 1-minute windows in Flink. 3. For each metric, run the ensemble: EWMA (detect level shifts), STL decomposition (detect seasonal pattern breaks), Isolation Forest (detect multivariate outliers across correlated metrics). 4. Label each data point as normal/attention/alarm. 5. If alarm, fire alert to PagerDuty with the anomalous metric and context (which dimension, how far from expected, recent trend). 6. Feedback loop: SRE confirms or dismisses alarm → system learns from feedback (reduce false positives).

[Interview Angle] Amazon L6: "Design an anomaly detection system for AWS infrastructure." Key difference from generic anomaly detection: AWS has millions of customers, so you need customer-level and account-level anomaly detection, not just aggregate. The system must handle seasonality (e.g., Black Friday traffic has different normal than Tuesday 3am), and must not flag legitimate usage spikes as anomalies. Answer: use STL decomposition to extract trend/seasonal/residual, run statistical tests on the residual, and use customer baselines that adapt weekly.
sections.codeBlock:
import numpy as np
from sklearn.ensemble import IsolationForest
from pyod.models.iforest import IForest
from pyod.models.knn import KNN
from pyod.models.lof import LOF
from pyod.models.combination import average, maximization
from typing import List

class AnomalyEnsemble:
    """Ensemble anomaly detector using PyOD-based models.
    ponytail: uses simple averaging; production needs learned weights.
    """
    def __init__(self, contamination: float = 0.01):
        self.detectors = [
            IForest(contamination=contamination, n_estimators=100),
            KNN(contamination=contamination, n_neighbors=10),
            LOF(contamination=contamination, n_neighbors=20),
        ]
        self._fitted = False

    def fit(self, X: np.ndarray) -> None:
        """Fit all detectors on baseline (normal) data."""
        for d in self.detectors:
            d.fit(X)
        self._fitted = True

    def predict(self, X: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
        """Ensemble prediction: average anomaly scores, then threshold."""
        scores = np.column_stack([d.decision_function(X) for d in self.detectors])
        # Average normalization — each detector's score is centered
        avg_score = average(scores)
        # Threshold: contamination percentile
        threshold = np.percentile(avg_score, (1 - 0.01) * 100)
        return (avg_score > threshold).astype(int)

    def stream_score(self, x: np.ndarray) -> float:
        """Real-time anomaly score for a single data point."""
        return np.mean([d.decision_function(x.reshape(1, -1)).item()
                        for d in self.detectors])

sections.title: Capacity Planning for ML Systems
sections.description:
[Intuition] Capacity planning is the art of predicting how much compute/gpu/memory/storage you need for an ML workload before you buy it. The key insight: ML inference has fundamentally different resource profiles than training. Inference needs low latency (so you over-provision), while training can tolerate higher utilization (batch throughput). For serving LLMs, the bottleneck is memory bandwidth (you compute one token at a time, but you load all parameters from HBM for each token). For training, compute is the bottleneck (matrix multiplications are compute-bound with large batch sizes).

[Concrete Numbers] Worked example: Llama-2-70B serving on A100-80GB. Model weights: 70B × 2 bytes (BF16) = 140GB. KV cache per token: 80 layers × 8 KV heads × 128 dim × 2 bytes × 2 (K+V) = ~330KB per token. For 4096-token context: ~1.3GB. Total memory per replica: 140GB (weights) + 1.3GB (KV cache) + ~10GB overhead = ~151GB. Fits on 2x A100-80GB with tensor parallelism = 2. If you want 100ms/token latency: A100 HBM bandwidth is 2TB/s, so weights load in 140GB / 2TB/s = 70ms, leaving 30ms for attention computation. Throughput on 2 GPUs: ~10 tokens/second per replica. For 1000 requests/second with 512-token output, you need ~1000 × 512 / 10 = 51,200 replicas (or batch requests 8x → ~6,400 replicas).

[Practical Workflow] 1. Profile the model: measure latency p50/p95/p99 for different batch sizes. 2. Estimate peak traffic: historical max QPS × 1.5 (headroom). 3. Calculate memory per replica: weights + KV cache + activations + framework overhead. 4. Calculate throughput per replica: tokens/second at target batch size. 5. Divide total traffic by throughput per replica to get minimum replicas. 6. Add factor of safety: 2× for HA (handle one AZ failure). 7. Plan GPU allocation: replicas × GPUs per replica, plus spare capacity for training jobs and experimentation.

[Interview Angle] Google L6: "How many GPUs do we need to serve ChatGPT-scale traffic?" The interviewer wants a structured Fermi estimation: (1) estimate daily active users and average queries per user → queries per second. (2) Estimate model size and tokens per query. (3) Calculate tokens/second per GPU based on memory bandwidth and model size. (4) Account for batching efficiency (batch size increases throughput). (5) Factor in redundancy (2-3x for HA). Show your work and state assumptions clearly.
sections.codeBlock:
def capacity_calculator(
    model_params_b: float = 70,         # Model size in billions
    bytes_per_param: int = 2,           # BF16
    gpu_hbm_gb: int = 80,              # A100 HBM
    gpu_bandwidth_tbps: float = 2.0,    # A100 HBM bandwidth TB/s
    target_latency_ms: float = 100,
    tokens_per_request: int = 512,
    peak_qps: int = 1000,
    redundancy_factor: float = 2.0,
    batch_size: int = 8,
) -> dict:
    """Calculate GPU requirements for LLM serving.
    ponytail: assumes optimal batching; real throughput may be lower."""
    # Model weight memory
    weight_gb = model_params_b * bytes_per_param / 1e9
    # KV cache per request (simplified: layers * heads * dim * 2 * tokens)
    kv_cache_gb = tokens_per_request * 330 * 1024 / 1e9  # ~330KB/token
    # Total memory per replica
    memory_per_replica_gb = weight_gb + kv_cache_gb + 10  # 10 GB overhead
    gpus_per_replica = int(np.ceil(memory_per_replica_gb / gpu_hbm_gb))
    # Time to load all weights (memory bandwidth bottleneck)
    weight_load_time_s = weight_gb / (gpu_bandwidth_tbps * 1e12 / 8)  # bytes / TB/s
    # Tokens per second per GPU (approximately)
    tokens_per_sec_per_gpu = 1.0 / (weight_load_time_s / gpus_per_replica)
    # Replicas needed
    req_per_sec = peak_qps * tokens_per_request / batch_size
    req_per_replica = tokens_per_sec_per_gpu * gpus_per_replica / tokens_per_request * batch_size
    min_replicas = int(np.ceil(req_per_sec / req_per_replica))
    total_gpus = int(min_replicas * gpus_per_replica * redundancy_factor)
    return {
        'weight_gb': round(weight_gb, 1),
        'kv_cache_gb': round(kv_cache_gb, 2),
        'memory_per_replica_gb': round(memory_per_replica_gb, 1),
        'gpus_per_replica': gpus_per_replica,
        'tokens_per_sec_per_gpu': round(tokens_per_sec_per_gpu, 1),
        'min_replicas': min_replicas,
        'total_gpus': total_gpus,
        'total_hbm_gb': round(total_gpus * gpu_hbm_gb, 0),
        'est_monthly_cost_usd': round(total_gpus * 8760 / 12 * 1.5, 0),
        # ^ $1.50/GPU-hour estimate for cloud A100
    }

sections.title: Design a Feature Engineering Pipeline
sections.description:
[Intuition] A feature engineering pipeline is the factory that produces training data — it takes raw data (user clicks, purchases, search queries) and transforms them into feature vectors. Two modes: batch features (computed on Spark daily/hourly, stored as Parquet files) and streaming features (computed on Flink from event streams, stored in a low-latency online store). The critical property is consistency: features available at training time must match features available at serving time. Point-in-time correctness is the hardest part: when you train on T-1 data, you must compute features using only data available up to the prediction time, not data from the future.

[Concrete Numbers] Batch pipeline: 100+ features per entity, 10B+ events daily → 10TB+ of raw data processed on Spark (500+ nodes, 1 hour window). Streaming pipeline: Kafka at 1M+ events/sec, Flink with 10-second windows → features updated with sub-minute latency. Cost comparison: batch feature computation ~$100-500/hour on cloud Spark; streaming ~$1000-5000/month per Flink job (depends on parallelism). Consistency: feature values at training vs serving should be within <0.1% difference — any discrepancy is a bug.

[Practical Workflow] 1. Data scientist specifies the feature: SQL query for batch, Flink job for streaming. 2. CI validates: runs on a sample, checks schema, computes statistics (min/max/mean/std/null rate). 3. Backfill: compute feature values for the last 90 days (point-in-time correct). 4. Online sync: batch results copied to online store (RocksDB/Cassandra). 5. Streaming pipeline populates real-time features (e.g., clicks in last 5 minutes). 6. Monitoring: feature freshness, null rate, distribution drift (daily PSI check). 7. Deprecation: feature is deprecated with a 30-day TTL — after that, it's no longer computed or served.

[Interview Angle] Meta E6: "Design a feature platform for Meta's ads ranking system." Meta's scale: billions of features, trillions of training examples. The key challenge is compute cost — a single cross-feature (user × ad × time) could be prohibitively expensive. Answer: (1) Use feature hashing to bound the feature space. (2) Automatically generate crosses from categorical features (like FM). (3) Retain features that pass a importance threshold (SHAP-based pruning). (4) Use a feature store with time-travel queries for point-in-time correctness. (5) Distinguish between real-time features (streaming) and historical features (batch).
sections.codeBlock:
from typing import Dict, List, Optional
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
import pandas as pd

class FeaturePipeline:
    """Unified feature pipeline: batch + streaming feature computation."""
    def __init__(self, feature_config: Dict):
        self.config = feature_config
        self.online_store = {}  # In production: RocksDB/Cassandra

    def compute_batch_features(self, date: datetime) -> pd.DataFrame:
        """Batch feature computation (Spark DF conceptually, pandas for demo)."""
        # 1. Load raw events for the date
        raw = self._load_events(date)
        # 2. Compute aggregated features per entity
        features = raw.groupby('entity_id').agg({
            'views': 'sum',
            'clicks': 'sum',
            'purchases': 'sum',
            'revenue': 'sum',
        }).reset_index()
        # 3. Compute ratios (avoid division by zero)
        features['ctr'] = features['clicks'] / (features['views'] + 1e-8)
        features['conversion_rate'] = features['purchases'] / (features['clicks'] + 1e-8)
        # 4. Add time-based features
        features['days_since_last_purchase'] = self._compute_recency(features['entity_id'])
        return features

    def compute_streaming_feature(self, event: Dict) -> float:
        """Real-time feature: clicks in last 5-minute sliding window."""
        entity_id = event['entity_id']
        now = datetime.now()
        window_start = now - timedelta(minutes=5)
        # In Flink: sliding window over event time
        recent_clicks = self._get_recent_events(entity_id, 'click', window_start, now)
        return len(recent_clicks)

    def get_features(self, entity_id: str, feature_names: List[str]) -> Dict:
        """Serve features for training or inference."""
        return {name: self.online_store.get(f'{entity_id}:{name}')
                for name in feature_names}

    def _load_events(self, date):
        return pd.DataFrame({'entity_id': ['a', 'b', 'c', 'a'],
                             'views': [10, 20, 5, 15],
                             'clicks': [1, 2, 0, 1],
                             'purchases': [0, 1, 0, 0],
                             'revenue': [0.0, 50.0, 0.0, 0.0]})

    def _compute_recency(self, entity_ids):
        return [10 - hash(e) % 10 for e in entity_ids]

    def _get_recent_events(self, entity_id, event_type, start, end):
        return []

sections.title: Design an ML Monitoring & Observability System
sections.description:
[Intuition] ML monitoring is a closed-loop feedback system. The output of predictions feeds back into the monitoring system, which triggers alerts, and the alerts trigger human investigation that leads to retraining or rollback. The core metrics are: data drift (have the input features shifted?), concept drift (has the relationship between features and target changed?), model staleness (how long since training?), prediction bias (are predictions systematically different from observed outcomes?), and data quality (null rate, schema violations, freshness). Each metric needs an alert threshold that balances false positives vs missed detections.

[Concrete Numbers] Data drift: PSI < 0.1 (no drift), 0.1-0.25 (monitor), > 0.25 (alert). Concept drift: accuracy drops by more than 2% from baseline. Model staleness: alert if model age > 2× the expected retraining interval (e.g., model trained 3 days ago but expected retrain is daily → alert). Prediction drift: mean prediction in last 1 hour differs from last 24-hour mean by > 2σ. Data quality: null rate > 5% or schema change detected on any feature. Alert fatigue mitigation: only page if 3 consecutive windows are anomalous.

[Practical Workflow] 1. Define monitoring config per model: which features to track, alert thresholds, expected retrain interval. 2. Aggregation pipeline: Kafka → Flink → 5-minute window aggregates. 3. Compute drift metrics: PSI for each feature, KS test for continuous, χ² for categorical. 4. Feed into alerting system: if 3 consecutive windows exceed threshold, fire PagerDuty. 5. Dashboard (Grafana or Evidently UI): show current vs reference distributions, prediction histogram, latency p50/p95/p99. 6. Incident response: on-call ML engineer investigates — was it a pipeline bug, data source change, or genuine drift? 7. Postmortem: update monitoring thresholds based on incident findings.

[Interview Angle] Google Staff: "Design ML monitoring for Google Search." Search has hundreds of models (ranking, spell correction, query understanding). The platform needs to monitor each model individually and also detect system-level issues (e.g., if all models show drift simultaneously, it's likely a data infrastructure problem, not model decay). Answer: hierarchical monitoring — model-level dashboards for each team, a system-level dashboard that aggregates per-model health, and auto-root-cause analysis that correlates drift across models with infrastructure changes.

sections.title: FAANG Staff+ Q&A — System Design
sections.description:
[Intuition] Staff+ Q&A scenarios test whether you can apply platform-scale thinking to concrete FAANG problems. Each question is a multi-system design where the answer reveals tradeoffs across ML infrastructure, experimentation, deployment, and monitoring. The common thread: you're not asked to design a model — you're asked to design the system around the model.

[Concrete Numbers] Across all 5 FAANG scenarios, key scale figures: Google Search (1000+ ML engineers, 10B+ daily queries), Meta Ads (10K+ concurrent experiments, billions of auction participants), Amazon Fulfillment (10M+ SKUs, 500+ warehouses), Azure (10K+ enterprise customers, each with their own models and SLAs), Apple (1B+ on-device inference requests daily). Every answer must quantify throughput, latency, cost, and team efficiency.

[Interview Angle] Q1 (Google L6) "Design a next-generation ML platform for Google Search." — This goes beyond feature store and training: it's about enabling 1000+ ML engineers to iterate quickly while maintaining production guarantees. Architecture: (1) Unified experiment framework — every change is an experiment, from model architecture to training data to inference infrastructure. (2) Auto-candidate generation — the platform suggests model improvements based on error analysis (clustering failure cases, identifying underperforming slices). (3) Automated deployment pipeline — if a candidate model improves metrics in shadow mode, it graduates to canary automatically. (4) Self-healing infrastructure — if prediction quality degrades, the platform auto-rolls back and notifies the owner. Tradeoff: developer velocity vs platform reliability. Too much automation and teams lose understanding of their systems.

[Interview Angle] Q2 (Meta E6) "Design the experimentation platform for Meta's Ads ranking system." — Meta runs 10K+ concurrent experiments on ads. The challenge is interference: changing the ad model for 5% of users changes the auction dynamics. Solution: (1) Switchback experiments for marketplace-level changes — randomize time intervals (30 min windows) rather than users. (2) Variance reduction via CUPED on advertiser spend (pre-experiment covariate). (3) Holdout sets: 1% of users never see experimental models, serving as a "clean" baseline against network effects. (4) Auto-stop experiments that cause revenue decline >0.5% with 95% confidence. The key metric isn't just CTR — it's total advertiser value (revenue, auction efficiency, advertiser retention).

[Practical Workflow] Each staff scenario follows a similar pattern: clarify requirements and scale, identify the bottleneck that prevents scaling, design the abstraction layer that removes the bottleneck, embed safety mechanisms (monitoring, rollback, governance), and quantify the tradeoff. Always state the key metric you're optimizing and the guardrail metrics you're protecting.

[Interview Angle] Q3 (Amazon L6) "Design an ML system for Amazon fulfillment center inventory optimization." — This is a supply chain ML problem with Staff+ scope because it spans multiple systems: demand forecasting (time-series), inventory placement (optimization), and logistics (routing). Architecture: (1) Demand forecasting per SKU-warehouse-day using DeepAR or temporal fusion transformer (10M+ SKUs, 500+ warehouses). (2) Inventory optimization: given demand forecasts, solve a constrained optimization (minimize shipping cost + holding cost, subject to service level targets). Use a differentiable optimizer (OptNet) or a learned optimization (predict the optimal inventory levels directly). (3) Logistics: predicted demand changes feed into the routing system to pre-position inventory. Key metric: in-stock rate vs inventory turns. Tradeoff: more inventory in more warehouses improves delivery speed but increases holding costs.

[Interview Angle] Q4 (Microsoft Partner) "Design an MLOps platform for Azure that serves 10K+ enterprise customers." — This is a cloud-scale challenge: each customer has different models, data, compliance requirements, and SLAs. Architecture: (1) Multi-tenant model registry with per-customer isolation (VNet, private endpoints). (2) Managed training infrastructure: customers provide data, Azure provides compute (ND-series VMs with A100/H100). (3) Auto-ML at scale: automated model selection, hyperparameter tuning, and deployment for common use cases (forecasting, classification, anomaly detection). (4) Responsible AI dashboard: fairness analysis, explainability (SHAP), error analysis, model interpretability built into the platform. (5) Compliance: SOC 2, HIPAA, GDPR — every operation logged, every model version retained for audit. Key tradeoff: ease-of-use vs flexibility — Auto-ML simplifies for beginners but frustrates advanced users. Solution: offer both with a path from Auto-ML to custom.

[Interview Angle] Q5 (Apple Staff) "Design the on-device ML system for Apple Intelligence (privacy-first, 1B+ devices)." — On-device ML has fundamentally different constraints: no cloud, limited compute/memory/battery, users expect instant results. Architecture: (1) On-device model training: federated learning with differential privacy (Apple's Private Federated Learning). Models are trained on-device, only encrypted gradient summaries are sent to Apple. (2) Model compression: quantization (FP16 → INT4 → INT2), pruning, distillation — compress the model to fit in <100MB while maintaining >95% of full-precision accuracy. (3) On-device inference with Core ML / ANE (Apple Neural Engine). (4) Model delivery via differential privacy — Apple pushes updated models to devices via a private set intersection protocol (only the relevant models for each device). (5) Hybrid execution: easy queries handled entirely on-device (latency <10ms), complex queries optionally routed to Apple's Private Cloud Compute (with verifiable transparency). Key tradeoff: model quality vs device compatibility — older devices have less capable neural engines. Solution: tiered models — Full model for Pro devices, distilled model for older devices, rule-based fallback for unsupported devices.

[Key Takeaway] Staff+ ML system design is about platform thinking — you design systems that enable other teams to build ML systems. The pattern is always: (1) Identify the bottleneck that prevents teams from scaling (feature inconsistency, training infra, monitoring). (2) Build a platform that abstracts the complexity while providing enough flexibility for customization. (3) Embed safety: monitoring, rollback, governance, and experiment guardrails are not optional — they're the core platform features. (4) Tradeoffs are expected and you should articulate them clearly: centralized vs self-serve, latency vs accuracy, cost vs quality, developer velocity vs reliability. (5) Always quantify: Staff+ interviews expect concrete numbers in every answer.

[Common Mistakes] (1) Designing a monolith: Staff+ scope requires modular platforms, not a single system that does everything. (2) Ignoring interference: A/B testing for ML systems has network effects — classic statistical assumptions break at scale. Acknowledge and address this. (3) Neglecting the human-in-the-loop: even the best ML platform needs humans for edge cases, labeling, and oversight. (4) No concrete numbers: "we need more GPUs" without a worked calculation is a red flag at Staff+ interviews. (5) Over-engineering: don't design a 3D parallelism system for a model that fits on one GPU. Start simple, then add complexity where needed. (6) Missing the feedback loop: every ML system degrades over time — the monitoring and retraining mechanism must be part of the design, not an afterthought.

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• System Design Interview (Alex Xu) — Comprehensive system design interview preparation
  • ML System Design Interview (Ali Aminian) — ML-specific system design interview guide
  • Google TFX — TensorFlow Extended: Google's production ML platform
  • Google MLOps Guide — Continuous delivery and automation for ML pipelines
  • Uber Michelangelo — Uber's ML platform architecture for large-scale ML
  • Meta Engineering Blog — Meta's engineering blog covering ranking, ads, recommendation systems
  • Netflix Tech Blog — Netflix's platform engineering and recommendation system insights
  • HNSW Paper — Efficient and robust approximate nearest neighbor search
  • FAISS Paper — Facebook AI Similarity Search: billion-scale similarity search
  • ZeRO: Memory Optimization for Training Large Models — Distributed training memory optimization for large models
  • FlashAttention — Fast and memory-efficient exact attention with IO-awareness
  • PagedAttention (vLLM) — Efficient memory management for LLM serving
  • Amazon DynamoDB — Fully managed NoSQL database for online feature serving
  • Google Spanner — Globally distributed, strongly consistent database for model registry
  • Apache Kafka — Distributed event streaming platform for real-time ML pipelines
